speaker: interviewer
text: Martin Kohn. Martin, spelled Martin. Roll one, Martin Kohn. [INAUDIBLE] [? MK: ?] I tell you, you're going to show this video next century, it might go. But this century-- [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS] [LAUGHS] Next century. They're going to show it in the next two centuries. [INAUDIBLE] Today, February 8th 1995. The survivor, Martin Kohn. The interviewer is Dana Schwartz. The city and state, Los Angeles, California, United States of America. And the language will be English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please state your name, and where and when you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Martin, Martin Kohn. And I was born in 1929 in Csaholc, Hungary, in a small village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about your grandparents, great-grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember my father's parents quite well. We spent lots of time, you know, in, with my grandparents, even though they lived in another town, another-- he had a farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [HUNGARIAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my grandfather was-- well, he was, like they say, he was a [YIDDISH] mensch with a big, white beard. He was a tall, tall person. And of course, you know, I remember their estate. And you know, we stayed, you know, with them on the farm, you know. I mean, actually, it wasn't-- there was farms connected to it. But I mean, this was mainly just sort of like a, an estate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do on that farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they raised cattle, and sheep, and all kinds of different granaries, potatoes, tobacco, and also had a winery and a distillery where they used to distill, you know, alcohol. And of course, right next to the estate, there was, you know, a small little distillery. They used to make, of course, you know, brandies from fruit, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had a big orchard, right connected to the estate-- you know, like apple trees, and pear trees, and plum trees, and of course walnut trees, and so on. And of course-- what do you call it? You know, on the adjoining place, they had these-- what do you call where you keep the wheat and tobacco? You know, I mean-- barns. Big, big barns. Of course, they had also other places which weren't connected-- adjacent, you know, which they had fields and all kind of different things. Sounds like a prosperous farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was considered a wealthy man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about his personality?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was a very tough personality, tough person. Because first of all, I mean, you know, like they had the-- anti-Semitism in Hungary was always very big. But I mean, he was the type of person who wasn't afraid of any one of them, you know? And spoke his mind, and you know? And nobody dared call him a Jew or insult him to his face. I mean, he was a self-made man. You know, after he was finished at yeshiva, came back and started working just as a farm hand, and worked himself up, you know, to the, to the, I mean, to the position that he--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Gershon [NON-ENGLIGH] or--

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kohn, K-O-H-N. Or in Hungarian, they called him Geza. Geza. They call him Geza.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife was, well, my grandmother. Her name was-- oh my goodness-- was Chaya Sarah, you know? We never called grandmothers by their first name. We just called her-- you know, but her name was Chaya Sarah. And also--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about her? Can you describe her for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, as I remember her, you know, she was already an older woman, you know, sort of bent, and constantly, you know, just used to roam around, do her chores, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have help in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had lots of helps, but not necessarily live-in help or anything. Because it was, like, you know, around the estate they were-- you know, the help, the hired help, all worked there. They needed, I suppose, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever stay with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vacations?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed. Yeah. Well, we used to when we were younger, especially when we lived in Hungary. We used to go for Passover, you know, for a couple of, couple of weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe Passover at grandmother's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, typical. I mean, of course, they had quite a few sons, and daughter, and everything else. And Passover was mainly, you know, my grandfather and our immediate family. And all the other children used to come in after the-- you know, because they used to conduct their own Seder with their own family. And they would-- you know, I had two uncles who lived also on the estate. And they would come in afterwards, you know, sort of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the Passover service by itself? How long did it take? What kinds of memories do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it will take-- of course, you know, just to tell the Haggadah-- you know, the story of Passover-- would probably take an hour and a half. Because there are always, you know, some discussions, some questions, and so on and so forth. It would take about probably an hour and a half to maybe even two hours until the meal was served.

speaker: interviewee
text: So actually, of course, I was you know, a small, small child. So I don't-- I can't recollect exactly till what time I was up. But I'm sure that it must have lasted into maybe 1, 2 o'clock in the morning. Was any of it fun for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, it was fascinating, you know. It was quite fascinating to, to be sort of with the family, you know? But I mean, I was quite young. I must have been only just about three, four years old, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, and then we moved to Romania, which was Satmar. Szatmarnemeti, but Satu Mare in Romanian. And movement wasn't so-- between Hungary and Romania it was a little more restricted. But we did go-- I think I remember in 1937, we went to my grandfather's for Passover. And of course, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the place that I was born, he was a farmer. As I could-- as I understand it right now, that must have been during the depression time, and things weren't so, going so rosy on the farm. So he just-- he went into Satmar. You know, he went in to open-- I suppose to learn the business of manufacturing. You know, small manufacturing. Like I mentioned to you, he was-- you know, from a jersey type of garments. They used to make, like, ski outfits and summer bathing suits, mainly ladies' bathing suits, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had machines that would make the fabric? Or he would buy the--

words.text: fabric? Or he would buy the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No. The cloth-- they would buy the cloth. You know, matter of fact, I had an uncle who used to manufacture the cloth. And we just-- you know, they used to cut it and sew it, you know-- that type of operation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your mother's parents at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were-- actually, my mother's parents, the eldest grandson was named after him. So he died approximately in 1923 or so. '21 or '23, I don't exactly remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My grandfather's name was Nachmen, Nachmen Braun. And they lived in Kisvarda, you know, also a Hungarian city. And my grandmother's name was Faigl.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't remember her?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I mean, I never-- matter of fact, I don't think I've ever seen a picture of my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what they did for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was-- also he was a-- manufacturing, I think, if I'm not mistaken. He was a manufacturer, like military-- what do you call it? Military, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uniforms. You know? And also he owned also a farm in the place that I was born. But they died, you know, during the-- in the early 1920s.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I also remember my father, my father's grandfather. I remember quite well. He was a man-- at that time he must have been-- they said that he was about 105 when he passed on. And that was in 1941. '40 or '41, thereabouts.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was a quiet, quiet man. I remember when, you know, I used to go, you know, to synagogue, you know, when we came to that particular town. And we went to the synagogue. And kids liked to go out to play.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, my father was the type-- he always used to like to keep us right next. And my father kind of wanted to punish me. He says, no. Go. Leave him alone. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, we used to go there on Saturdays, for instance. You know, he was already in the age and couldn't walk to the synagogue. He would walk for the morning prayer. He would walk to the temple, which was, I would say, I would say maybe a quarter-- a little bit better than a quarter of a mile. But at night, you know, towards the evening services Saturday night, we used to go to his house. And they conducted it over there. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name, the great-grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. There was-- oh, my goodness. Gershon, and his name was-- I think it was [NON-ENGLISH] Lazar, if I'm not mistaken. You know, I have to write these things down. Because, you know, they start, you know, to fade from your memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the old man died a natural death in '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes, in '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, his Jewish name was [? Leib ?] They caught him by the name of [? Leib. ?] But his Hungarian name was Lajos.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bella. Bella. Or in Yiddish, they called her Rivka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rivka. Tell me about them and the kind of household you lived in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember very little as far as, you know, on the farm. You know, I remember some, you know, which was very, very nice. You know? We didn't live quite on the farm, you know. But also, we had like-- it must have been an estate. You know, not as elaborate as my, my father's birthplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was very nice, you know? And we occupied-- there was, I think, maybe 11, 12, or 13 rooms in the-- you know, but we occupied only about maybe three, four, plus kitchen and things like that. How many siblings did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were seven, seven all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many boys and how many girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were four boys and three girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were the second?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the second, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about being in the family and your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the family was-- you know, I mean, I remember most of their births, you know? My brother and I and my two sisters were still born on the-- no. Yeah. I think we were born yet in the small town of Csaholc, you know? And the rest of the three children were born already in Satmar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they born at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were all born at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember the actual birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember. I mean, I don't remember my, my sister's birth-- the first one, my first sister. But my second sister I remember, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember the time that, you know, that she was born, you know, not really too many particular-- I mean, too many details.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you, do you remember your mother making noise?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I mean, I wasn't by the actual birth, you know. I didn't attend the birth, you know. But I remember, you know, the event, you know, that she was-- you know, she was born. And yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your family life. Did you eat together? Did you do things together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, we all-- of course, you know, let's say breakfast. You know, my mother used to fuss around, you know, about the girls, and us, you know, when we were smaller. And you know, send us off to school, give us breakfast. And you know, I mean, it was an apartment. You know, we had an apartment over there. And the kitchen, everything-- actually, everything mostly was conducted in the kitchen. And then there was a little, small little foyer or a den. And that's where things happened. And-- but yeah. Well, everything-- you know, I mean-- and then, of course, lunch. Lunch was-- everybody was home for lunch. And sometimes, of course, you know, there were certain days of school where they kept us a little longer. Then we ate a little later, you know, just ourselves, you know, the two older boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: But otherwise, you know, between 12:30 and 1:00 o'clock, you know, we used to all sit down and eat, eat lunch, what we called the mittag, you know? And, of course, it was quite eventful, because there was not only us. You know, there was always a cousin or two, or a guest. And if somebody came to the door and asked for, you know, if we could spare-- you know, so they ask him to sit down, you know? So actually, there was always about 12, 13, 14, or 15 just on an average day. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, also in the morning, if people came to the door, and asked for-- you know, they want to have breakfast, and you know, they served them breakfast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that happen often, that people would just knock on the door?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, all the time. You know? And if they didn't come--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were mainly people from other towns. Some were sort of beggars, you know, or whatever. Or, they came for money, or they came, or they asked you for-- you know, to have, if they wanted something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were treated well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about how you treated these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean-- you know, like for instance-- I mean, you know, like on a Saturday, on a holiday, people were invited. You know, like for instance after temple, if somebody came over to my father and said, can you spare lunch, or whatever, he would invite them to the house, one or two people. And they were sat at the table. They were served like everybody else, you know, and included into conversation and different things. And then of course, you know, there was always, you know, like students who were in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the yeshiva, you know. And then there was boys, you know, who had different days, you know? Like [? cast. ?] You know, like for instance, one had Monday. One had Tuesday. Wednesday, sometimes two. You know, and then we always had cousins, you know, who were in the yeshiva over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I had a cousin, of course, you know, who worked with my father in the business. And he was a steady-- ate in the house all the time. You know? That's why I say there was, almost every day there was 14, 15 people. You know? And then on holidays it was of course, sometimes-- or Saturdays, even more. What was the name of the yeshiva in the Satmar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was the Satmar Yeshiva. There was the Satmar Rebbe and the Satmar Yeshiva. He was considered-- in the Orthodox circles, he was a very well known rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet the Rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most certainly. I met him. Of course, we used to go to his services, not every Saturday. Occasionally on Saturdays, on high holidays we attended his services. And of course, even if we didn't attend, you know that we would go to his, you know-- what do you call the tish, the table. In other words, when, you know-- because there were so many people who ate with him, you know. And then everybody would come after. After everybody was finished at home, they would come, you know, to the activities-- whatever activities, sometimes dancing, you know, depending what kind of holiday it was, you know, like Passover or Sukkot. Of course, Simchas Torah it was another situation. Because Simchas Torah, everybody attended. Maybe 1,000 to 2,000 people were dancing in the, in the synagogue, you know. And the rabbi would go. You know, he would make a-- they make a circle. And he had a little Torah, you know. He would run around with the Torah. It was quite impressive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have to do with him? Do you remember any kind of a meeting with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not a meeting. But of course, you know, sometimes he would just go over and, you know, shake hands. But I-- I learned in his yeshiva, you know? So I sometimes twice a week would attend, you know, his-- you know, like an examination. You understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: You would stay in the line. And you would go, and they would ask you a certain passage from the Talmud, or a certain passage from, from the, from the Bible, you know, and its commentaries, you know? Then whatever he asked you, try to see how, how well you're doing, or you understand what-- you know. And I was quite young. I was only about 12, 12 and a half at that particularly time. Did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was bar mitzvah in, in '43. I remember. Or the end-- no. Wait a minute. 1929. I was eleven. No. I think I was bar mitzvah in 19-- or '42 or '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And times were already not the best. Of course, you know, the anti-Semitism was even in the '30s. You know, a kid could remember. You know, you couldn't walk on the street if you worked on, on, on you know, excluded places, you know? You also had to be afraid, you know, somebody shouldn't throw a stone at you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not so much the grown up people. But the younger generation, you know, always used to be very, very anti-Semitic. And of course, you know, you look Jewish. Because you know, you were wearing payos. And even if you didn't, they-- for some reason or other, they recognize you. And I don't think-- even if you weren't Jewish and they threw a stone at you, I don't think they were too sorry, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you felt the anti-Semitism because there was always conversation. In the synagogues, you know, people were talking, you know? So it was all around you all the time. What were they saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, you know, call you dirty Jews and all kind of, all kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they saying in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I mean-- the synagogue, they were discussing, I mean, different events, you know, that happened. You know, like for instance, in Romania at that particular time when they had the Iron Guard and so on and so forth, you know? And there was different cities. And where there was a pogrom or something, you know, or they, you know, ganged up, or they desecrated a temple, or something. You know? So there was always, you know, a conversation, in the-- you know, in the mikveh, mainly-- mikveh or in the temple, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a time, a special time in the '30s, that something happened to make anti-Semitism stronger?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean by making--

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean did come out? Or, did somebody--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, in Hungary. Romania wasn't as bad. You understand? In Romania, it started, I remember, when King Carol, you know, abdicated. And his son came, you know, and then the Iron Guard, you know, and then these different things. Of course, our city wasn't as-- how shall I say?

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, we weren't as involved because the population was more different population. You know? Like Germans, and Hungarian, and Serbs. And you know, it was more of a mixture of population.

speaker: interviewee
text: But places where it was, you know, populated or predominantly Hungarian or predominantly Romanian, of course, the Romanians in their country felt, you know, stronger. And these things was happening, you know, quite often.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, this was towards the end of 19-- the '30s, you know? Like '37, '38, when these things happened. And then of course the Hungarians came in 1940. They took over. And did it get worse when the Hungarians came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for a while it was OK, you know? But then, of course, you know, even though-- like for instance, our home town was a military town. You know, there was quite a few-- [NON-ENGLISH]? Or what do you call it, you know, where military people live? Camps, I suppose, you know, military camps. And neither one, you know, were afraid to-- with Jews, to start up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, you know, when the Hungarians came in and there was a lot of Romanian population, the Romanian population there-- their priests wear also beards, you know? Then for a while, when they came in they started-- you know, of course, a Jew, you know, would pull their beard or cut their beard. And I mean, it was almost an everyday event. But also-- Did you ever see any of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not necessarily. I mean, I've been chased many times by soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, many times we were chased by--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you win or did they win?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I was a little faster than they were. But it was an occasion, you know, that you had to really run for-- you know. And that's-- and of course, and as it, as it was going on year by year, you know, Hungarians, you know, of course, you know, got involved in the war with Russia. And then anti-Semitism, you know, grew, you know, tremendously. And of course, you know, then for a while, in the '40s, when the Hungarians came in, they took yet some Jews, you know, to the military, you know, and sent them to the front, or whatever. And but then later on, you know, they changed. And they didn't take them to military service, only to work details.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, of course, they were sending out different-- you know, to different places. To the front, you know, and different-- to Poland, and so on and so forth. And then of course, at that particular time-- 1943, I think, or maybe even sooner-- they, the Germans, were asking to Hungary, they should give out all the ones who are non-citizens, so to speak, who were born somewhere else, which a lot of them were considered Polish citizens, you know? And they took these families and took them over to the other side, you know, [ON-ENGLISH] and those. And I don't know if you ever heard of the Dnieper, the river? Dnieper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dnieper you know, where, you know, they, they did some killings and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? Near the River Dnieper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in that particular area, you know? I mean, I was a kid at that particular time, didn't-- but we knew about it. Because a lot of people, of course, came back, you know? And so it started quite early. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe some of the things that happened in your town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, different things, you know, like that happened. I can't recall exactly, you know, I mean, details. You know, there was-- you know, Jews were put into jail for different things regardless-- you know, false accusations of smuggling and dealing or whatever. And a lot of people, you know, because they might have not-- they hid themselves from not going into, into the military service. And, you know, some Jews were accused of espionage and all that kind of stuff, you know, where they were hauled away. And you know, I mean, this was conversations, you know, which was going on all the time.
